history.label: 3rd century
history.text: Turkmen historian Ovez Gundogdiyev believes that the name goes back to the Parthian era, 3rd century BC, deriving from the name of the founder of the Parthian Empire, Arsaces I of Parthia, in Persian.
From 1919 until 1927, the city was renamed Poltoratsk after a local revolutionary, Pavel Poltoratskiy.
Ashgabat is called Aşgabat (transliterated as "Ashgabat") in Turkmen, Russian: Ашхабад, romanized: Ashkhabad in Russian from 1925 to 1991, and عشق‌آباد ('Ešqābād) in Persian.
Before 1991, the name was usually spelled Ashkhabad in English, a transliteration of the Russian form.

history.label: Modern city
history.text: It has also been variously spelled Ashkhabat and Ashgabad.

history.label: Civic role
history.text: Although the name literally means "city of love" or "city of devotion" in modern Persian, the name might have been modified through folk etymology.
